scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Yesterday in ED majors I assessed a 62-year-old man with pleuritic chest pain. I considered ACS, PE and pneumonia, used Wells score, reviewed ECG and CXR, discussed with my consultant, and arranged CTPA. I learned to state my risk assessment more clearly when escalating.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a 62-year-old with pleuritic chest pain, formulated a differential diagnosis, used a decision tool (Wells score), reviewed investigations, and discussed the case with a consultant, making it a classic case for assessing clinical reasoning on a retrospective case record.

The trainee explicitly identifies a personal learning point ('I learned to state my risk assessment more clearly when escalating'), which warrants a structured reflection on their communication and escalation practice.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I assessed a 62-year-old man presenting with pleuritic chest pain. My differential diagnosis included acute coronary syndrome (ACS), pulmonary embolism (PE), and pneumonia.

To risk-stratify the patient, I calculated a Wells score, reviewed the electrocardiogram (ECG), and examined the chest X-ray (CXR). Based on my assessment, I discussed the case with the ED consultant and arranged a CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA) to definitively rule out a PE.
draft_fields.reflection:
I realised that I need to state my formal risk assessment and score results more clearly when escalating cases or discussing management plans with senior colleagues.

In future, I will explicitly communicate the calculated Wells score and pre-test probability at the start of my escalation to a shared understanding of clinical risk.
key_capabilities: SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. Today I performed DC cardioversion for unstable atrial fibrillation as a formal DOPS. My consultant observed each step directly. I checked consent, monitoring and sedation plan, delivered a synchronised shock, and reflected on calling for airway support earlier.

recommended_forms.rationale: Directly observed hands-on procedure with senior supervision; DOPS is the specific assessed-procedure form.
The performed procedure can also be recorded in the procedural log.
Feedback/reflection was included alongside the procedural assessment.

draft_fields.trainee_performance: I performed a synchronised DC cardioversion under direct consultant observation for a DOPS assessment. I systematically checked patient consent, ensured appropriate continuous monitoring was in place, and established a clear sedation plan before safely delivering the synchronised shock.
draft_fields.reflection: The procedure was successful, but on reflection, I should have called for senior airway support earlier in the preparation phase. While the sedation and shock delivery went smoothly, airway support is bedside before starting mitigates risk if the patient's airway becomes compromised.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC2: be expert in fluid management and circulatory support in critically ill patients (2025 Update)
SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.form_name: Direct Observation of Procedural Skills - ACCS
scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. During my ACCS anaesthetic placement I inserted a chest drain using Seldinger technique as an observed DOPS on 5 July 2026. My supervisor watched the procedure. I confirmed landmarks, used sterile technique and reflected on slowing down before dilating.

recommended_forms.rationale: ACCS procedural skill with direct observation; use the ACCS DOPS form.
The same ACCS procedure can also be recorded in the ACCS procedural log.

draft_fields.case_observed: I inserted a chest drain using the Seldinger technique under the direct observation of my supervisor. I confirmed the anatomical landmarks, maintained a strict sterile technique throughout, and performed the procedure step-by-step.
draft_fields.reflection:
During the procedure, I reflected on the importance of slowing down and controlled movements, particularly before dilating the tract. 

Taking that extra moment to verify depth and alignment before advancing the dilator patient safety and prevents tract misplacement. I will continue to apply this deliberate pause in future invasive procedures.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. A consultant directly observed me taking a focused history and examination from a 7-year-old with wheeze in paediatric ED. I explained the plan to the parent and received feedback on structuring my assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee was directly observed by a consultant while performing a focused history, clinical examination, and communicating the management plan to a paediatric patient's parent, which fits the Mini-CEX criteria for direct observation of a clinical encounter.

draft_fields.clinical_reasoning: I took a focused history and performed a clinical examination on a 7-year-old presenting with a wheeze in the paediatric ED.
draft_fields.reflection: I was directly observed by a consultant during this assessment. I explained the management plan to the child's parent and received constructive feedback from the consultant on how to better structure my paediatric assessment.
key_capabilities: SLO5 KC1: be expert in assessing and managing all children and young adult patients attending the ED (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. On an evening acute take I managed six patients across majors and resus, including sepsis, chest pain and a frail fall. My consultant observed my prioritisation, handover and escalation decisions over the shift.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed multiple acute patients across majors and resus during an evening shift, with a consultant observing their prioritisation, management, and escalation decisions.

draft_fields.cases_observed: During an evening acute take, I managed six patients across majors and resus. The cases included a presentation of sepsis, acute chest pain, and a frail patient who had sustained a fall.
draft_fields.reflection:
This shift required me to manage multiple competing priorities across different areas of the department. Balancing the acute resuscitation needs of the sepsis patient while safe assessment and workup for the chest pain and frail fall patients challenged my clinical prioritisation.

My consultant observed my decision-making, handover, and escalation processes throughout the shift. This feedback confirmed that my communication with the wider team was clear and my safety netting was appropriate.

In future shifts, I will continue to focus on early escalation and structured handovers during high-volume periods to maintain departmental flow and patient safety.
key_capabilities: SLO2 KC2: aware of when it is appropriate to review patients remotely or directly and able to teach these principles to others (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I acted as EPIC for a crowded evening ED shift. I allocated junior doctors, held a safety huddle, escalated capacity concerns to site management and reflected on protecting time for re-review of high-risk patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee took on a formal leadership role as EPIC (Emergency Physician in Charge) during a crowded shift, managing resources, leading a safety huddle, and coordinating with site management.
The trainee actively reflects on the shift, specifically identifying the need to protect clinical time for the re-review of high-risk patients during periods of high crowding.
The trainee managed the department and prioritised resources across multiple patients during an acute, high-density shift.

draft_fields.leadership_context: I acted as EPIC (Emergency Physician in Charge) during a crowded evening shift. The department was under significant capacity pressure, requiring active management of junior doctor allocations, team communication, and site-level escalation.
draft_fields.clinical_reasoning:
I took on the role of EPIC to manage the flow and safety of the department during a high-pressure evening shift.

My key actions included allocating junior doctors to appropriate clinical areas to balance the workload, holding a departmental safety huddle to align the team, and actively escalating capacity concerns to the hospital site management team.
draft_fields.reflection:
Managing a crowded department highlighted the challenge of maintaining oversight while under constant operational pressure.

I realised the importance of consciously protecting my time to perform re-reviews of high-risk patients, rather than becoming entirely absorbed by administrative flow decisions. In future busy shifts, I will establish structured intervals to step back from the flow desk specifically to review complex or high-risk cases with the junior team.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC2: will be able to liaise with the rest of the acute/urgent care team and wider hospital as shift leader (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I appraised evidence on using age-adjusted D-dimer for suspected PE. I framed a PICO question, searched NICE and a recent meta-analysis, and discussed how I would apply the evidence safely in low-risk patients. I learned to document the limits of the evidence when explaining risk to patients.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee formulated a structured PICO question, performed a literature search of guidelines and a meta-analysis, and critically appraised the evidence to guide clinical practice.

The trainee includes clear reflective elements, specifically identifying what they learned regarding the limitations of the evidence and how they will alter their future documentation and risk-communication practice.

draft_fields.situation: I appraised the evidence surrounding the clinical utility and safety of using age-adjusted D-dimer cut-offs for patients with suspected pulmonary embolism (PE).
draft_fields.pico_population: Patients presenting with suspected pulmonary embolism (PE)
draft_fields.pico_outcome: Diagnostic safety, sensitivity, and rate of unnecessary CT pulmonary angiograms (CTPA)
draft_fields.search_methodology: I searched NICE guidelines and identified a recent meta-analysis addressing age-adjusted D-dimer cut-offs.
draft_fields.evidence_evaluation: I evaluated the evidence regarding the diagnostic accuracy of age-adjusted D-dimer thresholds compared to standard thresholds in low-risk patients, noting its potential to safely reduce unnecessary imaging.
draft_fields.apply_to_practice: I discussed how to apply this evidence safely to help rule out PE in low-risk patients without resorting to unnecessary CTPA imaging, while maintaining diagnostic safety.
draft_fields.communicate_to_patient: I discussed the importance of documenting the limits of the evidence clearly when explaining diagnostic uncertainty and clinical risk to patients.
draft_fields.reflection:
This appraisal highlighted the utility of age-adjusted D-dimer thresholds in reducing unnecessary diagnostic imaging. 

I learned that while the evidence supports this practice in low-risk cohorts, I must remain diligent in documenting the specific limits of the evidence when explaining diagnostic risk and safety margins to patients.
key_capabilities: SLO10 KC1: be able to appraise, synthesise, communicate and use research evidence to develop EM care (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I delivered a 25-minute teaching session to four junior doctors on ECGs in chest pain. A consultant observed the session and gave feedback on pacing and checking understanding.

recommended_forms.rationale: Formal teaching session with observation/assessment stated explicitly.

key_capabilities: SLO9 KC2: be able to prepare and deliver teaching sessions outside of the clinical environment, including simulation, small group work, and didactic presentations (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I reviewed feedback from nurses, juniors and consultants. Themes were calm leadership and clear explanations, with a development point around documenting senior discussions more consistently.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee has gathered and reviewed feedback from multiple professional groups (nurses, juniors, consultants) regarding their overall clinical performance, leadership, and documentation, which directly aligns with the multi-source feedback process.

The trainee is reflecting on specific constructive feedback themes (calm leadership, clear explanations, and the need to document senior discussions more consistently) to guide their professional development.

draft_fields.context: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee. I reviewed my Multi-Source Feedback (MSF) containing feedback from nurses, junior doctors, and consultants.
draft_fields.reflection:
The feedback was reassuring, particularly around themes of maintaining calm leadership on shift and providing clear explanations to team members.

As a development point, the feedback to document senior discussions more consistently in patient notes. I will focus on all consultant escalations and key management decisions are clearly documented with names and times.

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. Baseline audit was 42 cases, we introduced a triage prompt and re-audit showed improvement from 58 to 39 minutes. I presented it at governance. My PDP summary this year is to improve quality improvement leadership. Next year I will focus on embedding the prompt and supporting re-audit.

recommended_forms.rationale: QI/audit project with measurement and change; QIAT is the specific assessment form.

draft_fields.qi_engagement: I led a QI project on time to antibiotics in adult sepsis. I completed a baseline audit of 42 cases, introduced a triage prompt as an intervention, and conducted a re-audit to measure change. I then presented the findings at the departmental governance meeting.
draft_fields.qi_understanding: This developed my practical understanding of using baseline measurement, an intervention and re-audit to assess change in an ED quality improvement project.
draft_fields.reflection:
Our baseline audit of 42 cases identified delays in administering antibiotics for adult sepsis. By introducing a simple triage prompt, we successfully reduced the time to antibiotics from 58 to 39 minutes. Presenting these findings at the departmental governance meeting allowed me to share the clinical impact with the wider team.

This project demonstrated how targeted, front-door interventions can improve delivery of time-critical therapy. I learned that engaging the triage nursing team early was essential to embedding the prompt into our workflow.
draft_fields.next_pdp: Next year I will focus on embedding the triage prompt further and supporting a subsequent re-audit to the improvements are sustained.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC1: be able to provide clinical leadership on effective Quality Improvement work (2025 Update)
SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I presented a journal club on CT coronary angiography in low-risk chest pain. I summarised the paper, discussed validity and explored how it might change local ED pathways.

recommended_forms.rationale: Journal club presentation or discussion described explicitly.

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I taught two F1 doctors and a medical student a structured approach to ECG interpretation using five anonymised ECGs from the teaching file. I collected verbal feedback afterwards.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee delivered an informal, opportunistic teaching session on ECG interpretation to junior colleagues using teaching file cases, which was not formally observed by a senior assessor.
Allows the trainee to reflect on the educational delivery, the verbal feedback received from the learners, and how they would adapt their teaching style for future sessions.

draft_fields.learning_outcomes: How to apply a structured approach to ECG interpretation using anonymised clinical cases.
key_capabilities: SLO9 KC2: be able to prepare and deliver teaching sessions outside of the clinical environment, including simulation, small group work, and didactic presentations (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I performed a shoulder reduction in ED with senior advice available but no formal DOPS. I documented neurovascular status before and after and reflected on analgesia planning.

recommended_forms.rationale: Trainee-performed procedure without a clear direct-assessment signal.

draft_fields.reflective_comments:
I performed a shoulder reduction in the emergency department with senior advice available. 

I that I documented the patient's neurovascular status both before and after the manipulation to guard against complications. I also took time to reflect on the analgesia planning to the patient remained comfortable and the muscles were sufficiently relaxed to a smooth reduction.

I will continue to rigorous pre- and post-procedural neurovascular documentation and structured analgesia planning for all joint reductions I perform.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. On ACCS I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision. I checked contraindications, used sterile technique, obtained CSF samples and reflected on patient positioning.

recommended_forms.rationale: ACCS procedural skill without a clear direct-assessment signal; use the ACCS procedural log.

draft_fields.brief_description: I performed a lumbar puncture under supervision on an ACCS placement. Prior to the procedure, I systematically checked for contraindications and prepared a sterile field. I successfully obtained CSF samples for analysis.
draft_fields.reflective_comments:
The procedure went well, but it reinforced the critical importance of patient positioning in a successful first-pass attempt. Optimising the patient's spinal flexion and they remain aligned perpendicular to the bed improves anatomical landmark identification.

In future, I will spend more time preparation-wise the patient is fully comfortable and correctly positioned before opening the sterile pack, as this reduces patient discomfort and procedural time.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)
SLO9 KC1: be able to undertake training and supervision of members of the ED team in the clinical environment (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways, then applied the learning by reviewing our local traffic-light guidance and changing my safety-netting.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee completed a self-directed learning module on paediatric fever pathways independently.
The trainee reviewed and applied local traffic-light clinical guidance to update safety-netting practices, which is a management portfolio task.
The trainee reflects on how they applied this new learning to adapt and improve their clinical practice and safety-netting.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Paediatric Fever Pathways and Safety Netting
draft_fields.resource_details: RCEMlearning module on paediatric fever pathways, alongside a review of local traffic-light guidance for febrile children.
draft_fields.reflection:
Yesterday, I completed a self-directed learning module focusing on paediatric fever pathways. I followed this up by reviewing our local department's traffic-light guidance to my clinical practice aligns with current local protocols.

I reflected on how I integrate these structured risk-stratification tools into my daily practice. The module of safety-netting, which prompted me to refine how I deliver and document safety-netting advice to parents and carers of febrile children.

I have adapted my practice to safety-netting is more structured and clearly documented in the patient notes, referencing specific red flags from the traffic-light system so that parents know exactly when to represent.
key_capabilities: SLO5 KC1: be expert in assessing and managing all children and young adult patients attending the ED (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I performed lung ultrasound on a breathless patient with suspected pulmonary oedema. I identified bilateral B-lines, discussed images with my consultant and reflected on integrating ultrasound with clinical assessment.

recommended_forms.rationale: Point-of-care ultrasound case is stated explicitly.

draft_fields.clinical_scenario: I assessed a breathless patient with suspected acute pulmonary oedema.
draft_fields.how_used: I performed a point-of-care lung ultrasound to look for features of pulmonary congestion.
draft_fields.usable_images: I was able to obtain usable images, identifying bilateral B-lines.
draft_fields.interpret_images: I interpreted the presence of bilateral B-lines as supportive of pulmonary congestion and discussed these images with my consultant.
draft_fields.learning_points: I reflected on the value of integrating point-of-care ultrasound findings directly with my clinical assessment to guide the management of acute dyspnoea.
key_capabilities: SLO6 KC2: the knowledge and psychomotor skills to perform EM procedural skills safely and in a timely fashion (2025 Update)

SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I reflected on a complaint about delayed communication with a family during a busy resus shift. The clinical care was appropriate, but I should have allocated a clearer update role and documented conversations better.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee is directly reflecting on a patient/family complaint about communication and documentation, focusing on lessons learned and future practice adjustments.
The trainee is processing a personal reflection on communication gaps, cognitive load during a busy shift, and how to improve non-technical skills in the future.

If the trainee is also analyzing this complaint from a formal departmental management perspective (e.g., conducting a review of communication pathways during resus), this satisfies the ST3-6 Management Portfolio requirements.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflection on complaint regarding communication during busy resuscitation shift
draft_fields.key_features: I reflected on a complaint received regarding delayed communication with a family during a busy resuscitation shift.
draft_fields.key_aspects: The clinical care provided to the patient was appropriate. However, during a highly active resuscitation environment, there was a failure to establish a timely and clear line of communication with the family.
draft_fields.learning_points: I learned that even when clinical management is sound, communication must be structured and prioritised. I need to explicitly delegate the family update role to a specific team member during busy resuscitation scenarios rather than assuming it will occur organically. I must also all family updates, or attempts to update them, are clearly documented in the medical records.
draft_fields.further_action: I will change my practice during resuscitation cases by assigning a dedicated staff member to communicate with and support the relatives early in the presentation. I will also a clear summary of family discussions is documented in the notes.
key_capabilities: SLO7 KC1: have expert communication skills to negotiate, manage complicated or evolving interactions (2025 Update)
SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC6: demonstrate a positive impact on the culture of the Emergency Department through attitudes and behaviours that impact positively on colleagues, patients and their relatives (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I contributed to a serious incident review after delayed recognition of sepsis in majors. I reviewed the timeline, identified handover gaps and learned to use an explicit trigger for senior review.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee actively contributed to a formal serious incident review regarding delayed recognition of sepsis, identifying systemic gaps and personal learning.
The trainee reflects on their learning from the incident, specifically noting handover gaps and the need to implement an explicit trigger for senior review.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Serious Incident Review: Delayed Recognition of Sepsis
draft_fields.description: I contributed to a serious incident review following the delayed recognition of sepsis in a patient who was located in the majors area of the Emergency Department.
draft_fields.root_causes: After reviewing the timeline of the patient's journey, we identified critical gaps during the handover process which led to a delay in recognizing the severity of the patient's condition.
draft_fields.contributing_factors: The contributing factors included communication failures and lack of continuity during shift handovers in a busy majors department.
draft_fields.learning_points: I learned the importance of handover structures. I have learned to implement and use an explicit trigger for senior review when clinical parameters deteriorate or when a diagnosis remains unclear during transition times.
draft_fields.further_action: I will apply these learning points to my daily handover practice, explicit safety netting and clear triggers for senior review are communicated for high-risk patients.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)
SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I attended a regional paediatric emergency medicine teaching day covering safeguarding, sepsis and non-accidental injury. I reflected on using the learning in future paediatric assessments.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee attended an educational activity as a learner.

draft_fields.title_of_education: Regional paediatric emergency medicine teaching day
draft_fields.learning_points: I attended this regional teaching day covering safeguarding, sepsis, and non-accidental injury in paediatric patients. I reflected on how to apply these updated clinical and safeguarding principles to my future paediatric assessments in the Emergency Department.

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. I completed ATLS today. The course refreshed my trauma team approach, primary survey and communication with the team leader. I will apply the structured handover in resus.

recommended_forms.rationale: Formal course attendance/completion is stated explicitly.

draft_fields.project_description: I completed the Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) course today.
draft_fields.reflective_notes: The course refreshed my trauma team approach, focusing on the structured primary survey and maintaining clear communication with the trauma team leader. It was a valuable opportunity to practice coordinating roles within a high-pressure resuscitation environment.
draft_fields.resources_used: Advanced Trauma Life Support (ATLS) course material and practical scenarios.
draft_fields.lessons_learned: I learned to refine my approach to the primary survey and the importance of structured communication during trauma resuscitations. I will apply the structured handover in the resuscitation room.
key_capabilities: SLO4 KC1: be expert in assessment, investigation and clinical management of patients attending with all injuries, regardless of complexity (2025 Update)

SLO3 KC3: manage all the life-threatening conditions including peri-arrest & arrest situations in the ED (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. My consultant assessed me during a three-hour evening ED shift. I managed flow in majors, supported juniors, reviewed two unwell patients and reflected on maintaining situational awareness under pressure.

recommended_forms.rationale: Directly matches a formal, multi-hour shift observation of non-technical skills (flow, supporting juniors, situational awareness) by a consultant assessor.
The trainee explicitly reflects on maintaining situational awareness and managing cognitive load under pressure during a busy shift.
Suitable for evaluating the clinical management, prioritisation, and oversight of multiple patients across a defined acute shift period.

draft_fields.reflection:
During this three-hour evening shift, I acted as the shift lead under consultant observation. I focused on managing flow in the majors area, supporting junior colleagues with clinical queries, and directly reviewing two unwell patients who required urgent escalation.

Maintaining overall situational awareness while diving into individual reviews of unstable patients is always a delicate balance. I had to consciously step back after each clinical review to re-assess the departmental overview, that ambulance handovers and waiting times did not deteriorate while my attention was focused.

I will continue to practice this deliberate pacing on busy shifts, I explicitly delegate the oversight of department flow to the nurse in charge when I must step away to manage critically unwell patients.
key_capabilities: SLO8 KC1: will provide support to ED staff at all levels and disciplines on the ED shift (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC3: will maintain situational awareness throughout the shift to ensure safety is optimised (2025 Update)
SLO8 KC4: will anticipate challenges, generate options, make decisions and communicate these effectively to the team as lead clinician (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Voice transcript: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. Senior review prompted a PE workup. I learned to pause when the story does not fit and to document my differential more explicitly.

recommended_forms.rationale: The trainee managed a breathless patient and discussed their clinical reasoning, cognitive bias (anchoring), and differential diagnosis management retrospectively with a senior colleague.
The trainee explicitly reflects on their diagnostic reasoning, identifying an anchoring bias on COPD, and details how they will change their future documentation and diagnostic approach.

draft_fields.reflection_title: Reflecting on anchoring bias in a breathless patient
draft_fields.reflection:
I managed a breathless patient where I initially anchored on COPD. I focused on this respiratory history during my initial assessment and started management down this pathway.

My senior review prompted a PE workup instead, as some clinical features did not fully align with a simple COPD exacerbation. This intervention redirected the clinical path to exclude a pulmonary embolism.
draft_fields.replay_differently: I would initiate a deliberate re-evaluation pause to challenge my working diagnosis when the clinical picture does not fully fit.
draft_fields.why: I experienced anchoring bias on the patient's known history of COPD, which narrowed my initial differential diagnosis and delayed consideration of alternative causes for breathlessness.
draft_fields.different_outcome: The clinical outcome was successful due to the senior intervention, but maintaining a broader differential earlier would have expedited the correct workup and reduced the risk of diagnostic delay.
draft_fields.focussing_on: I am focussing on incorporating a structured diagnostic pause into my assessments of patients with chronic respiratory conditions, explicitly writing down and ruling out life-threatening mimics like pulmonary embolism.
draft_fields.learned: I have learned how easily clinical history can lead to cognitive anchoring. I now recognise the importance of actively seeking out features that do not fit a suspected exacerbation to avoid premature diagnostic closure.
key_capabilities: SLO2 KC2: aware of when it is appropriate to review patients remotely or directly and able to teach these principles to others (2025 Update)

SLO2 KC1: able to support the pre-hospital, medical, nursing and administrative team in answering clinical questions and in making safe decisions for patients with appropriate levels of risk in the ED (2025 Update)
SLO1 KC1: to be expert in assessing and managing all adult patients attending the ED. These capabilities will apply to patients attending with both physical and psychological ill health (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. A consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries. They fed back that my explanations were clear but I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first.

recommended_forms.rationale: Teaching was observed for feedback on the trainee's teaching skill.
The same activity can also be recorded as teaching delivered.

draft_fields.reflection:
An Emergency Medicine consultant observed me teaching an F2 doctor how to assess ankle injuries.

My explanations were clear, but the observer fed back that I should check the learner's baseline knowledge first. In future, I will start teaching sessions by asking what the learner already knows to tailor the explanation better.

scenario.source_text: Document extract from a portfolio note: I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I completed an audit of documentation of capacity assessments in ED mental health presentations. I reviewed 30 notes, presented results and proposed a documentation prompt.

recommended_forms.rationale: Audit activity is stated without a full QI change/re-audit cycle.

draft_fields.reflection:
I completed an audit of the documentation of capacity assessments in ED mental health presentations, reviewing 30 patient notes. This allowed me to assess our current standards against clinical guidelines and identify gaps in how we record these critical assessments during acute presentations.

After reviewing the data, I presented the results to the department and proposed a documentation prompt to help improve compliance. This project highlighted how easily essential details can be omitted in a busy clinical environment and the value of structured tools to support frontline staff.

I plan to re-audit the department after the documentation prompt has been implemented to see if there is a measurable improvement in the quality of our capacity documentation.
key_capabilities: SLO11 KC2: be able to support and develop a culture of departmental safety, and good clinical governance (2025 Update)

SLO12 KC2: be able to investigate a patient safety incident, participate and contribute effectively to department clinical governance activities and risk reduction processes (2025 Update)

scenario.source_text: Context supplied with image: the image is supporting evidence only; do not infer findings beyond this text. I am an ST5 Emergency Medicine trainee on my Emergency Medicine placement. The activity happened yesterday. I recruited patients to an ED research study after completing GCP training. I checked eligibility, explained the study, obtained consent and reflected on protecting clinical flow.

recommended_forms.rationale: Research activity with recruitment/consent/study participation is stated explicitly.

draft_fields.reflection: Yesterday, I recruited patients to an ED research study. To prepare for this, I completed my Good Clinical Practice (GCP) training, I understood the ethical and regulatory requirements of clinical research. During the shift, I checked patient eligibility against the study criteria, explained the trial details clearly to suitable candidates, and obtained formal informed consent. This required balancing the administrative and communication demands of research recruitment with the necessity of maintaining clinical flow in a busy department. I learned how to discuss research participation clearly and efficiently with patients without disrupting their clinical journey or the wider department's operations.
key_capabilities: SLO10 KC2: be able to actively participate in research (2025 Update)
